sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Current bordered objects and rings:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] >= 12 and n['color'] in ('W', 'G', 'O', 'p'):
        center_row = sum(b[0] for b in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        center_col = sum(b[1] for b in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} ~({center_row},{center_col}) children={n['children']}")

# Check for white objects
print("\nWhite objects:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W':
        center_row = sum(b[0] for b in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        center_col = sum(b[1] for b in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} ~({center_row},{center_col})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:
Now I see:
- White-bordered at (51,40) with 12 pixels - this is the orange ring that now has a white border!
- Orange-bordered magenta moved from (20,44) to (23,41)
- Purple-bordered magenta still at (41,49)
- Gray-bordered at (9,49), (11,34), (35,45), (48,54)
- White HUD band at (42,0)
So clicking the orange ring:
1. Made the orange ring white-bordered
2. Moved the orange-bordered magenta from (20,44) to (23,41) - it moved DOWN along the trail toward the ring!
This is a big discovery! Clicking the ring makes the ring white-bordered and moves the magenta toward it.
Now the question is: what's the goal? Maybe I need to get the magenta INTO the ring? Or maybe I need to make all rings white-bordered?
Let me try clicking the purple ring to see if it does the same thing for the purple-bordered magenta.
